title: Add tokio async runtime foundation
analysis:
Currently reify-runtime has a SequentialScheduler (lib.rs:48-79) that iterates eval_set in order using a synchronous NodeEvaluator trait (lib.rs:35-46) with `fn evaluate(&mut self, node: &NodeId) -> EvalOutcome` and `fn is_dirty(&self, node: &NodeId) -> bool`. All identity types (ValueCellId, ConstraintNodeId, etc.) are composed of String and u32, so they are Send + Sync automatically. NodeId and EvalOutcome are also Send + Sync.

For the concurrent scheduler, we need: (1) tokio async runtime for spawning tasks, (2) tokio-util for CancellationToken, (3) an AsyncNodeEvaluator trait with `&self` (not `&mut self`) since concurrent evaluation requires shared access with interior mutability, (4) a ConcurrentScheduler that groups eval_set nodes by topological level and spawns all nodes within a level concurrently.

The topological level computation follows the same Kahn's algorithm as dirty.rs::topological_sort but outputs `Vec<Vec<NodeId>>` (batched by level) instead of flat `Vec<NodeId>`. This requires DependencyTrace information, so ConcurrentScheduler::execute takes `traces: &HashMap<NodeId, DependencyTrace>` in addition to the eval_set.

The AsyncNodeEvaluator trait uses RPITIT (Return Position Impl Trait In Traits), available since Rust 1.75 and natively supported in edition 2024 (this project's edition). The method signature `fn evaluate(&self, node: NodeId) -> impl Future<Output = EvalOutcome> + Send` captures the `'_` lifetime of `&self` implicitly in edition 2024, allowing the future to be awaited inside a tokio::spawn block that owns an Arc<E>.

New code goes in a new file `crates/reify-runtime/src/concurrent.rs` to keep the existing SequentialScheduler code untouched. The module is declared as `pub mod concurrent` in lib.rs.

CancellationToken is a thin wrapper around tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken, providing a project-specific API surface that decouples from the transitive dependency.

prerequisites.description: Add workspace dependencies to Cargo.toml: tokio = { version = "1", features = ["rt-multi-thread", "macros", "sync"] } and tokio-util = { version = "0.7", features = ["rt"] }. Add tokio.workspace = true and tokio-util.workspace = true to reify-runtime/Cargo.toml [dependencies].
Create crates/reify-runtime/src/concurrent.rs as an empty module file. Add `pub mod concurrent;` to lib.rs. Verify the crate compiles with `cargo check -p reify-runtime`.

steps.description: Write failing test `cancellation_token_create_and_cancel` in concurrent.rs: Create CancellationToken::new(), assert !is_cancelled(), call cancel(), assert is_cancelled(). Also test child_token(): create child from parent, cancel parent, assert child.is_cancelled(). Uses #[test] (not async — these are synchronous operations).

Implement CancellationToken wrapper struct in concurrent.rs. Fields: inner: tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken. Methods: new(), cancel(), is_cancelled(), child_token() -> Self, async cancelled(). Derive Clone. Implement Default delegating to new().

Write failing test `async_node_evaluator_mock_compiles` in concurrent.rs: Define a MockAsyncEvaluator struct with all_dirty: bool and result: EvalOutcome. Implement AsyncNodeEvaluator: is_dirty returns self.all_dirty, evaluate returns self.result. Write a #[tokio::test] that creates the mock, calls evaluate(node).await, asserts EvalOutcome::Changed. This test verifies the trait is usable and correctly defined.

Define AsyncNodeEvaluator trait in concurrent.rs. Import std::future::Future. Trait: `pub trait AsyncNodeEvaluator: Send + Sync { fn is_dirty(&self, node: &NodeId) -> bool; fn evaluate(&self, node: NodeId) -> impl Future<Output = EvalOutcome> + Send; }`. Uses RPITIT (edition 2024). No async_trait crate needed.

Write failing test `concurrent_scheduler_empty_eval_set` in concurrent.rs: Create ConcurrentScheduler, empty eval_set, MockAsyncEvaluator wrapped in Arc, empty traces HashMap, CancellationToken. Call execute().await. Assert returned HashSet is empty. Uses #[tokio::test].

Define ConcurrentScheduler as a unit struct in concurrent.rs. Implement execute method: `pub async fn execute<E: AsyncNodeEvaluator>(&self, eval_set: Vec<NodeId>, evaluator: Arc<E>, traces: &HashMap<NodeId, DependencyTrace>, cancel: &CancellationToken) -> HashSet<NodeId>`. Initial implementation: return empty HashSet for empty eval_set. Import Arc from std::sync, HashMap from std::collections, HashSet, NodeId, EvalOutcome, DependencyTrace.

Write failing test `concurrent_scheduler_single_dirty_node` in concurrent.rs: Create eval_set with one node (NodeId::Value(ValueCellId::new('A','x'))). MockAsyncEvaluator returns Changed for all, is_dirty=true. Empty traces (node has no deps). Call execute().await. Assert changed set contains the single node. Fails because execute returns empty.

Implement basic level computation and single-level execution in ConcurrentScheduler::execute. Add private fn compute_levels(eval_set: &[NodeId], traces: &HashMap<NodeId, DependencyTrace>) -> Vec<Vec<NodeId>> using Kahn's algorithm (same approach as dirty.rs::topological_sort but emitting levels). For execution: iterate levels, for each node in level check is_dirty, spawn tokio tasks for dirty nodes, join all handles, collect Changed results.

Write failing test `concurrent_scheduler_multi_level_ordering` in concurrent.rs: Build eval_set with 3 nodes: a (param), b (param), c (let, reads a and b). Traces: a and b have empty reads, c reads [a_vcid, b_vcid]. Use a TrackingAsyncEvaluator that records evaluation order in Arc<Mutex<Vec<NodeId>>>. After execute().await: assert all 3 nodes evaluated, assert c's position in eval_order is after both a and b (level ordering). Assert changed set has all 3.

Verify multi-level execution works with the compute_levels implementation from step-8. The Kahn's algorithm should correctly place a and b at level 0 (in-degree 0) and c at level 1 (depends on a and b). Level 0 nodes spawn concurrently, all join, then level 1 starts. Fix any issues found.

Write test `concurrent_scheduler_skips_non_dirty` in concurrent.rs: eval_set with 2 nodes (a, b). MockAsyncEvaluator where is_dirty returns true only for a, evaluate returns Changed. After execute, assert changed set contains only a (b was skipped). Verifies dynamic dirty check mirroring SequentialScheduler behavior.
Verify non-dirty skip works. The implementation from step-8 should already check is_dirty before spawning. If not, add the is_dirty check before spawning the tokio task for each node.

Write test `concurrent_matches_sequential_bracket_topology` in concurrent.rs: Build bracket-like topology with 4 nodes: width (param), thickness (param), volume (let, reads width+thickness), C1 (constraint, reads width+thickness). All dirty, all Changed. Run SequentialScheduler::execute with equivalent sync MockNodeEvaluator and ConcurrentScheduler::execute with async mock. Assert both produce the same changed set (all 4 nodes). Verifies behavioral equivalence.
Verify ConcurrentScheduler produces identical results to SequentialScheduler. Both should return the same changed set regardless of execution order within levels. Fix any discrepancies.

Write failing test `cancellation_stops_evaluation` in concurrent.rs: Build eval_set with 2 levels: level 0 has node a, level 1 has node b (depends on a). Use a CancellingAsyncEvaluator that cancels the CancellationToken during evaluation of node a. After execute().await: assert a was evaluated (in changed set or tracked), assert b was NOT evaluated (evaluator tracks eval count per node via AtomicUsize). The ConcurrentScheduler should check cancel between levels.

Add cancellation check in ConcurrentScheduler::execute: at the start of each level iteration, check if cancel.is_cancelled() and break if so. This ensures that once cancellation is triggered during level N's evaluation, level N+1 is skipped. Return whatever changed nodes were collected so far.

design_decisions.decision: New concurrent.rs module rather than extending lib.rs
design_decisions.rationale: lib.rs already has 343 lines including SequentialScheduler and tests. Adding ConcurrentScheduler, AsyncNodeEvaluator, CancellationToken, and async tests would make it unwieldy. A separate module provides clean separation while keeping SequentialScheduler as the fallback exactly as it is.

design_decisions.decision: RPITIT for AsyncNodeEvaluator::evaluate return type instead of async_trait crate or boxed futures
design_decisions.rationale: The project uses edition 2024 which fully supports Return Position Impl Trait In Traits. The signature `fn evaluate(&self, node: NodeId) -> impl Future<Output = EvalOutcome> + Send` avoids heap allocation (no Box<dyn Future>), avoids a crate dependency (no async-trait), and is idiomatic Rust 2024. The Send bound on the returned future ensures compatibility with tokio::spawn.

design_decisions.decision: AsyncNodeEvaluator takes &self (not &mut self) and evaluate takes NodeId (owned, not &NodeId)
design_decisions.rationale: &self instead of &mut self because concurrent evaluation shares the evaluator across multiple spawned tasks via Arc<E>. Mutable access would require synchronization that's better handled by the implementor with interior mutability (Mutex, RwLock, etc.). Owned NodeId instead of &NodeId avoids lifetime issues when moving data into spawned tasks — the node is cloned into each task's closure.

design_decisions.decision: ConcurrentScheduler::execute takes traces parameter for level computation
design_decisions.rationale: The ConcurrentScheduler needs dependency information to group nodes into topological levels. Rather than requiring the caller to pre-compute levels, execute accepts the trace map (already available from compute_eval_set) and computes levels internally using Kahn's algorithm. This keeps the API similar to SequentialScheduler (takes eval_set) while adding the minimal extra information needed for concurrency.

design_decisions.decision: Cancellation checked between levels, not within a level
design_decisions.rationale: Within a level, all tasks are spawned and joined together. Cancelling mid-level would require aborting spawned tasks, which adds complexity and may leave evaluator state inconsistent. Checking cancellation between levels is simpler, deterministic, and sufficient for the use case: cancel before starting expensive downstream levels. Individual task cancellation (e.g., via tokio::select! with the cancel token) can be added in a future refinement.

design_decisions.decision: CancellationToken as a thin wrapper type, not a re-export
design_decisions.rationale: A wrapper provides API stability: if we later switch from tokio-util to a custom cancellation mechanism (e.g., for WASM targets or testing), callers don't need to change. The wrapper exposes only the methods we need: new(), cancel(), is_cancelled(), child_token(), cancelled(). It also provides a natural extension point for logging or metrics.

design_decisions.decision: Level computation uses Kahn's algorithm mirroring dirty.rs::topological_sort
design_decisions.rationale: The same algorithm is used but outputs Vec<Vec<NodeId>> (levels) instead of Vec<NodeId> (flat). Each 'wave' of Kahn's algorithm (all nodes with in-degree 0 at that step) forms one level. Nodes within a level have no dependencies on each other and can safely execute concurrently. This is a minimal adaptation of proven existing code.

reuse.how: The compute_levels function follows the same algorithm: compute in-degree map, use BTreeSet for deterministic ordering, process nodes in waves. The key difference is emitting entire waves as levels (Vec<Vec<NodeId>>) instead of individual nodes (Vec<NodeId>). Same deduplication of reads via HashSet to avoid over-counting in-degree.

reuse.what: NodeId, EvalOutcome, DependencyTrace types from reify-eval
reuse.how: ConcurrentScheduler uses these types in its execute method signature. reify-runtime already depends on reify-eval. Import via `use reify_eval::cache::{EvalOutcome, NodeId}; use reify_eval::deps::DependencyTrace;`.

The existing tests define inline mock structs (NoopEvaluator, AlwaysChanged, CutoffEvaluator) for testing SequentialScheduler. Apply the same pattern for AsyncNodeEvaluator mocks: MockAsyncEvaluator, TrackingAsyncEvaluator, CancellingAsyncEvaluator. Each is a struct with the test-specific behavior (is_dirty policy, evaluate outcome, tracking state via Arc<Mutex<>>).
Create test NodeId values: NodeId::Value(ValueCellId::new('A','x')). Same pattern as existing SequentialScheduler tests.

ConcurrentScheduler follows the same behavioral contract: skip non-dirty nodes, call evaluate for dirty nodes, collect Changed nodes into HashSet. The API differs in being async, taking Arc<E> instead of &mut dyn, and additionally requiring traces for level computation.
